session.user:
# /erk:plan-implement
Implement a plan - either from GitHub, a markdown file, an existing impl directory under `.erk/impl-context/`, or by saving the current plan first.
This is the primary implementation workflow - it orchestrates:
1. Setting up the impl directory under `.erk/impl-context/<branch>/` (from issue, file, existing folder, or fresh plan) 2. Executing the implementation 3. Running CI and submitting the PR
## Prerequisites
- Must be in a git repository managed by erk - GitHub CLI (`gh`) must be authenticated - One of: - A plan number, URL, or file path argument - An existing impl directory under `.erk/impl-context/` - A plan branch checked out (e.g., `plnd/...` or `P{number}-...`) - A plan in `~/.claude/plans/` (from plan mode)
## Usage
```bash /erk:plan-implement # Use impl directory, detect from branch, or save current plan /erk:plan-implement 2521 # Fetch and implement PR #2521 /erk:plan-implement https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/2521 # URL form /erk:plan-implement ./my-plan.md # Implement from local markdown file ```
---
## CRITICAL: PR Operations
**NEVER** run direct GitHub CLI commands for PR operations during implementation:
- `gh pr edit` — destroys plan-header metadata block - `gh pr ready` — bypasses lifecycle tracking - `gh pr close` / `gh pr merge`
All PR operations are handled by `erk pr submit` in Step 13. It automatically:
- Generates the PR title and body - Preserves the plan-header metadata block - Marks draft PRs as ready for review - Updates lifecycle stage
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Set Up Implementation
Parse `` and run the consolidated setup command:
- **If numeric** (e.g., `2521`): `erk exec setup-impl --issue 2521` - **If GitHub URL** (e.g., `https://github.com/.../issues/2521`): Extract number, `erk exec setup-impl --issue 2521` - **If path to file** (anything else non-empty): `erk exec setup-impl --file <path>` - **If empty**: `erk exec setup-impl` (auto-detects from `.erk/impl-context/`, branch, or fails)
```bash erk exec setup-impl [--issue <N> | --file <path>] ```
This single command handles:
- Fetching plan from GitHub (draft PR) - Setting up from a local markdown file - Auto-detecting from existing impl directory under `.erk/impl-context/` - Auto-detecting plan number from branch name (P{number}-... or PR lookup) - Creating/checking out the feature branch - Creating impl directory under `.erk/impl-context/<branch>/` with plan content - Running impl-init validation - Cleaning up `.erk/impl-context/` staging directory (git rm + commit + push)
Otherwise, generate a branch slug and set up from the specified issue:
1. Fetch the title: `gh pr view <ISSUE_ARG> --json title -q .title` 2. Generate a branch slug from the title: - 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Examples: "fix-auth-session", "add-plan-validation", "refactor-gateway-abc" 3. Store as `BRANCH_SLUG`.
```bash erk exec setup-impl --issue <ISSUE_ARG> --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" ```
If `setup-impl` exits with code 1 and `error: "no_plan_found"`, fall back to saving the current plan:
Generate a branch slug from the plan title (the first `# ` heading in the plan file):
- 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Store as `BRANCH_SLUG`
Save the current plan to GitHub and capture the plan number:
```bash erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="9409898c-c91e-431d-a975-8a65cdb45b91" --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" ```
Parse the JSON output to get `pr_number`, then:
```bash erk exec setup-impl --issue <pr_number> ```
The `setup-impl` output includes `related_docs` (skills and docs to load) and `has_plan_tracking` (whether GitHub issue tracking is active).
If this fails, display the error and stop.
### Step 2: Read Plan and Load Context
Read `plan.md` from the impl directory set up in Step 1 to understand:
- Overall goal and context - Context & Understanding sections (API quirks, architectural insights, pitfalls) - Implementation phases and dependencies - Success criteria
**Context Consumption**: Plans contain expensive discoveries. Ignoring `[CRITICAL:]` tags, "Related Context:" subsections, or "DO NOT" items causes repeated mistakes.
### Step 3: Load Related Documentation
If plan contains "Related Documentation" section, load listed skills via Skill tool and read listed docs.
### Step 4: Create TodoWrite Entries
Create todo entries for each phase from the plan.
### Step 5: Signal GitHub Started
```bash erk exec impl-signal started --session-id="9409898c-c91e-431d-a975-8a65cdb45b91" 2>/dev/null || true ```
This also deletes the Claude plan file (from `~/.claude/plans/`) since:
- The content has been saved to GitHub - The content has been snapshotted to `.erk/scratch/` - Keeping it could cause confusion if the user tries to re-save
### Step 6: Execute Each Phase Sequentially
For each phase:
1. **Mark phase as in_progress** (in TodoWrite) 2. **Read task requirements** carefully 3. **Implement code AND tests together**: - Load `dignified-python-313` skill for coding standards - Load `fake-driven-testing` skill for test patterns - Follow project AGENTS.md standards 4. **Mark phase as completed** (in TodoWrite) 5. **Report progress**: changes made, what's next
**Important:** `plan.md` is immutable - NEVER edit during implementation
### Step 7: Report Progress
After each phase: report changes made and what's next.
### Step 8: Final Verification
Confirm all tasks executed, success criteria met, note deviations, summarize changes.
### Step 9: Signal GitHub Ended
```bash erk exec impl-signal ended --session-id="9409898c-c91e-431d-a975-8a65cdb45b91" 2>/dev/null || true ```
### Step 10: Upload Session for Async Learn
```bash erk exec upload-impl-session --session-id="9409898c-c91e-431d-a975-8a65cdb45b91" 2>/dev/null || true ```
This reads plan reference from the impl directory, captures session info, and uploads for async learn processing.
### Step 11: Clean Up Impl Context
Remove the `.erk/impl-context/` directory from the repo:
```bash erk exec cleanup-impl-context ```
This removes `.erk/impl-context/`, stages the deletion, commits, and pushes — all idempotent.
### Step 12: Run CI Iteratively
1. If `.erk/prompt-hooks/post-plan-implement-ci.md` exists: follow its instructions 2. Otherwise: check CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md for CI commands
### Step 13: Submit PR
Push the branch and create or update the PR using the Graphite-aware submit pipeline:
```bash erk pr submit ```
This handles pushing commits, creating/updating the PR, generating the PR description, and enhancing with Graphite stack metadata when available.
After successful submission, signal lifecycle transition:
```bash erk exec impl-signal submitted 2>/dev/null || true ```
Then validate PR completion invariants:
```bash erk pr check --stage=impl ```
This validates PR submission readiness including implementation-specific checks (e.g., `.erk/impl-context/` must be cleaned up). If checks fail, display output and warn user.
### Step 14: Output Format
- **Start**: "Setting up implementation..." or "Fetching plan #X..." - **After setup**: "Implementation environment ready, reading plan..." - **Each phase**: "Phase X: [brief description]" with code changes - **End**: "Plan execution complete. [Summary]"
---
## Related Commands
- `/erk:plan-save` - Save plan only, don't implement (for defer-to-later workflow) - `/erk:replan` - Re-plan an existing plan with current codebase state
session.assistant: Setting up implementation...

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: Auto-detect and set up implementation from current branch

session.assistant: Implementation environment ready. Reading plan...
session.tool_result:
1 # Plan: Add `metadata.internal: true` + improve skills documentation routing 2 3 ## Context 4 5 Skills in this repo appeared on the skills.sh public leaderboard. While investigating, we found that `docs/learned/capabilities/npx-skill-management.md` already documents the distinction between authored and npx-installed skills — but it wasn't discovered because nothing in AGENTS.md routes to it. The existing doc is good; the problem is discoverability. 6 7 ## Part 1: Add `metadata.internal: true` to authored skills 8 9 Add `metadata.internal: true` to all 20 SKILL.md files in `.claude/skills/`. The 5 symlinked skills in `.agents/skills/` are installed from external repos and should NOT be modified here. 10 11 ```yaml 12 metadata: 13 internal: true 14 ``` 15 16 **Files (20 total) — all `.claude/skills/`:** 17 ci-iteration, cli-skill-creator, cmux, command-creator, dignified-code-simplifier, erk-diff-analysis, erk-exec, erk-gt, erk-planning, erk-skill-onboarding, gh, learned-docs, npx-skills, objective, pr-feedback-classifier, pr-operations, refac-cli-push-down, refac-module-to-subpackage, rename-swarm, session-inspector 18 19 ## Part 2: Fix documentation routing in AGENTS.md 20 21 ### 2a. Add skills/capabilities row to the routing table (~line 170) 22 23 Add to the "Topic Area | Check First" table: 24 25 ``` 26 | Skills, SKILL.md, capabilities | `docs/learned/capabilities/` | 27 ``` 28 29 **File:** `AGENTS.md` line ~178 (after the TUI row) 30
... [33 lines omitted] ...

tool_use.name: TaskCreate
tool_use.param: Add metadata.internal: true to 20 authored SKILL.md files
Add metadata.internal: true to all 20 SKILL.md files in .claude/skills/. Do NOT modify the 5 symlinked skills in .agents/skills/.

session.tool_result: Task #1 created successfully: Add metadata.internal: true to 20 authored SKILL.md files

tool_use.name: TaskCreate
tool_use.param: 2a: Add skills/capabilities row to routing table. 2b: Add routing to Skill Loading Behavior section. 2c: Add description to capabilities category in docs/learned/index.md.

session.tool_result: Task #2 created successfully: Fix documentation routing in AGENTS.md

tool_use.name: TaskCreate
tool_use.param: Add section covering what metadata.internal: true does, why authored skills use it, and that NPX-managed skills are controlled by source repos.

session.tool_result: Task #3 created successfully: Add metadata.internal section to npx-skill-management.md

tool_use.name: TaskCreate
tool_use.param: Run verification checks from the plan, then run CI iteratively.

session.tool_result: Task #4 created successfully: Verify changes and run CI

tool_use.name: TaskCreate
tool_use.param: Clean up impl context, submit PR via erk pr submit, signal completion.

session.tool_result: Task #5 created successfully: Submit PR
Updated task #1 status

session.assistant: Good - 20 files in `.claude/skills/` and 5 in `.agents/skills/`. Let me check a few to understand the frontmatter format.
session.tool_result: 1 --- 2 name: ci-iteration 3 description: > 4 This skill should be used when running CI checks iteratively and fixing failures. Use when 5 executing make targets (fast-ci, all-ci, ci), iterating on lint/format/type/test errors, or 6 needing the devrun agent pattern for pytest/ty/ruff/prettier/make/gt commands. 7 --- 8 9 # CI Iteration 10 11 ## Overview 12 13 Run the specified CI target and automatically fix any failures. Keep iterating until all checks pass or you get stuck on an issue that requires human intervention. 14 15 **IMPORTANT**: All `make` commands must be run from the repository root directory. The Makefile is located at the root of the repository, not in subdirectories.

1 --- 2 name: gh 3 description: This skill should be used when working with GitHub CLI (gh) for pull requests, issues, releases, and GitHub automation. Use when users mention gh commands, GitHub workflows, PR operations, issue management, or GitHub API access. Essential for understanding gh's mental model, command structure, and integration with git workflows. 4 --- 5 6 # GitHub CLI (gh) 7 8 ## Overview 9 10 GitHub CLI (`gh`) is the official command-line tool for GitHub that brings pull requests, issues, releases, and other GitHub concepts to the terminal. This skill provides comprehensive guidance for using `gh` to streamline GitHub workflows, including PR management, issue tracking, repository operations, and API access. 11 12 ## When to Use This Skill 13 14 Invoke this skill when users: 15

1 --- 2 name: erk-exec 3 description: > 4 Guide for erk exec subcommands. Use when running erk exec commands 5 to understand syntax, find the right command for a task, or learn 6 common workflows. Always check syntax with -h or load this skill 7 before running erk exec commands. 8 --- 9 10 # erk exec Guide 11 12 ## Quick Start 13 14 Before running any `erk exec` command, check syntax with `-h`: 15

session.assistant:
None of those matches are `metadata:` as a YAML frontmatter field — they're all in-content mentions. So none of the 20 files have `metadata.internal` yet. I need to add `metadata:\n internal: true` before the closing `---` in each frontmatter.
Let me do this efficiently with a script:

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: for dir in .claude/skills/*/; do skill_file="${dir}SKILL.md"; if [ -f "$skill_file" ]; the...[truncated 556 chars]...adata: {path}') else: print(f'Skipped (no frontmatter): {path}') " "$skill_file"; fi; done
Add metadata.internal: true to all 20 SKILL.md frontmatters

session.tool_result: Exit code 1 (eval):1: parse error near `\n'

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param:
python3 << 'PYEOF' import os, glob
skill_files = sorted(glob.glob(".claude/skills/*/SKILL...[truncated 634 chars]...ed (no frontmatter): {path}")
print(f"\nTotal files processed: {len(skill_files)}") PYEOF
Add metadata.internal: true to all 20 SKILL.md frontmatters

tool_use.param: echo "=== .claude/skills with internal: true ===" && grep -rl "internal: true" .claude/ski...[truncated 20 chars]... with internal: true ===" && grep -rl "internal: true" .agents/skills/ 2>/dev/null | wc -l

session.tool_result: 1 --- 2 name: ci-iteration 3 description: > 4 This skill should be used when running CI checks iteratively and fixing failures. Use when 5 executing make targets (fast-ci, all-ci, ci), iterating on lint/format/type/test errors, or 6 needing the devrun agent pattern for pytest/ty/ruff/prettier/make/gt commands. 7 metadata: 8 internal: true 9 --- 10

Updated task #2 status
session.assistant: **Phase 2: Fix documentation routing in AGENTS.md and index.md**
session.tool_result: 140 - **Pathlib always**: Never `os.path`. Check `.exists()` before `.resolve()` 141 - **Absolute imports only**: `from erk.config import X`, never `from .config import X` 142 - **No re-exports**: Empty `__init__.py`, one canonical import path per symbol 143 - **Lightweight `__init__`**: No I/O in constructors. Use `@classmethod` factory methods for heavy operations 144 - **Properties must be O(1)**: No I/O or iteration in `@property` or `__len__`/`__repr__` 145 - **No backwards compatibility**: Break and migrate immediately, no legacy shims 146 - **Max 4 indentation levels**: Extract helpers to reduce nesting 147 148 ## Skill Loading Behavior 149 150 Skills persist for the entire session. Once loaded, they remain in context. 151 152 - DO NOT reload skills already loaded in this session 153 - Check skill documentation for backend-specific invocation patterns 154 155 **npx skills:** Use `-a claude` for Claude Code skills. The `.agents/` canonical directory covers Codex. Do NOT use `--all` — this repo does not support Windsurf or Cursor. 156 157 ## Documentation-First Discovery 158 159 Before launching Plan or Explore agents, search for relevant documentation: 160 161 1. **Scan the embedded index above** — match your task against the read-when conditions 162 2. **Grep docs/learned/** — extract keywords from your task and search: 163 - `Grep(pattern="keyword", path="docs/learned/", glob="*.md")` 164 - Use multiple searches if the task spans domains (e.g., both "gateway" and "testing") 165 3. **Read every matching doc** before writing code or launching Plan agents 166 4. **Pass discovered docs as context** when launching Plan agents — include file paths and key findings in the agent prompt 167 168 This grep step is mandatory for ALL coding tasks. It costs milliseconds and prevents re-learning lessons already documented. 169

165 3. **Read every matching doc** before writing code or launching Plan agents 166 4. **Pass discovered docs as context** when launching Plan agents — include file paths and key findings in the agent prompt 167 168 This grep step is mandatory for ALL coding tasks. It costs milliseconds and prevents re-learning lessons already documented. 169 170 | Topic Area | Check First | 171 | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | 172 | Session logs, ~/.claude/ | `docs/learned/sessions/` | 173 | CLI commands, Click | `docs/learned/cli/` | 174 | Testing patterns | `docs/learned/testing/` | 175 | Hooks | `docs/learned/hooks/` | 176 | Planning, .erk/impl-context/ | `docs/learned/planning/` | 177 | Architecture patterns | `docs/learned/architecture/` | 178 | TUI, Textual | `docs/learned/tui/`, `docs/learned/textual/` | 179 180 **Anti-pattern:** Skipping the grep because the task "seems simple" 181 **Anti-pattern:** Going straight to source files without checking docs/learned/ 182 **Correct:** Grep docs/learned/, read matches, THEN plan or code 183 184 ## Worktree Stack Quick Reference
session.assistant: Now I can see the exact locations. Let me also read the index.md capabilities line:
session.tool_result: 8 9 ## Categories 10 11 - [architecture/](architecture/) — Explore when working on core patterns (dry-run, gateways, subprocess, shell integration). Add docs here for cross-cutting technical patterns. 12 - [capabilities/](capabilities/) 13 - [changelog/](changelog/) 14 - [checklists/](checklists/) 15 - [ci/](ci/) 16 - [claude-code/](claude-code/) 17 - [cli/](cli/) — Explore when building CLI commands or output formatting. Add docs here for Click patterns and terminal UX. 18 - [commands/](commands/) — Explore when creating or optimizing slash commands. Add docs here for command authoring patterns. 19 - [config/](config/) 20 - [configuration/](configuration/) 21 - [documentation/](documentation/) — Explore when structuring or writing documentation. Add docs here for documentation methodology and best practices. 22 - [erk/](erk/) — Explore when working with erk-specific workflows (worktrees, PR sync, Graphite). 1 --- 2 title: NPX Skill Management 3 read_when: 4 - "adding a new skill to erk" 5 - "migrating a bundled skill to npx distribution" 6 - "understanding how skills-lock.json works" 7 - "debugging skill installation issues" 8 - "removing a skill from bundled distribution" 9 tripwires: 10 - action: "installing a new skill without updating _UNBUNDLED_SKILLS registry" 11 warning: "Skills managed by npx must be added to _UNBUNDLED_SKILLS in src/erk/capabilities/skills/bundled.py. Without this, erk will try to install the skill from its own bundle (where it doesn't exist) instead of from npx." 12 - action: "migrating a skill from bundled to npx without removing from pyproject.toml force-include" 13 warning: "npx-managed skills that remain in pyproject.toml force-include will be double-installed. Remove the force-include entry after migrating." 14 --- 15 16 # NPX Skill Management 17 18 Erk uses [npx skills](https://agentskills.io) (Vercel Labs, `skills@1.4.5`) to distribute skills from external GitHub repositories. This supplements erk's own bundled skills. 19 20 ## Directory Structure 21 22 - **`.agents/skills/`** — canonical installation directory for npx-managed skills 23 - **`.claude/skills/`** — Claude Code reads skills from here; contains symlinks to `.agents/skills/` entries 24 25 The symlinks ensure Claude Code picks up npx-managed skills alongside bundled ones. 26 27 ## skills-lock.json 28 29 **Source**: `skills-lock.json` (repo root) 30
... [61 lines omitted] ...

## metadata.internal
The `metadata.internal: true` frontmatter field hides a skill from `...[truncated 484 chars]...me: my-skill description: ... metadata: internal: true --- ```
